title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Two: In which Sophie is compelled to seek her fortune (Fragment 5)
text:
She hobbled over to the shop door and carefully put up the CLOSED notice. Her joints creaked as she moved. She had to walk bowed and slow. But she was relieved to discover that she was quite a hale old woman. She did not feel weak or ill, just stiff. She hobbled to collect her shawl, and wrapped it over her head and shoulders, as old women did. Then she shuffled through into the house, where she collected her purse with a few coins in it and a parcel of bread and cheese. She let herself out of the house, carefully hiding the key in the usual place, and hobbled away down the street, surprised at how calm she still felt.

She did wonder if she should say goodbye to Martha. But she did not like the idea of Martha not knowing her. It was best just to go. Sophie decided she would write to both her sisters when she got wherever she was going, and shuffled on, through the field where the Fair had been, over the bridge, and on into the country lanes beyond. It was a warm spring day. Sophie discovered that being a crone did not stop her enjoying the sight and smell of may in the hedgerows, though the sight was a little blurred. Her back began to ache. She hobbled sturdily enough, but she needed a stick. She searched the hedges as she went for a loose stake of some kind.

Evidently her eyes were not as good as they had been. She thought she saw a stick, a mile or so on, but when she hauled on it, it proved to be the bottom end of an old scarecrow someone had thrown into the hedge. Sophie heaved the thing upright. It had a withered turnip for a face. Sophie found she had some fellow feeling for it. Instead of pulling it to pieces and taking the stick, she stuck it between two branches of the hedge, so that it stood looming rakishly above the may, with the tattered sleeves on its stick arms fluttering over the hedge.

“There,” she said, and her cracked old voice surprised her into giving a cracked old cackle of laughter. “Neither of us are up to much, are we, my friend? Maybe you’ll get back to your field if I leave you where people can see you.” She set off up the lane again, but a thought struck her and she turned back. “Now if I wasn’t doomed to failure because of my position in the family,” she told the scarecrow, “you could come to life and offer me help in making my fortune. But I wish you luck anyway.”

She cackled again as she walked on. Perhaps she was a little mad, but then old women often were.

She found a stick an hour or so later when she sat down on the bank to rest and eat her bread and cheese. There were noises in the hedge behind her: little strangled squeakings, followed by heavings that shook may petals off the hedge. Sophie crawled on her bony knees to peer past leaves and flowers and thorns into the inside of the hedge, and discovered a thin gray dog in there. It was hopelessly trapped by a stout stick which had somehow got twisted into a rope that was tied round its neck. The stick had wedged itself between two branches of the hedge so that the dog could barely move. It rolled its eyes wildly at Sophie’s peering face.

As a girl, Sophie was scared of all dogs. Even as an old woman, she was quite alarmed by the two rows of white fangs in the creature’s open jaws. But she said to herself, “The way I am now, it’s scarcely worth worrying about,” and felt in her sewing pocket for her scissors. She reached into the hedge with the scissors and sawed away at the rope round the dog’s neck.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Two: In which Sophie is compelled to seek her fortune

vocabulary.definition: to cause worry or fear; feeling frightened or worried
vocabulary.example: Even as an old woman, she was quite alarmed by the two rows of white fangs.

vocabulary.definition: not clear or easy to see
vocabulary.example: Though the sight was a little blurred.

vocabulary.definition: very thin so that the bones show
vocabulary.example: Sophie crawled on her bony knees.

vocabulary.definition: to laugh loudly and unpleasantly, often like a bird’s call
vocabulary.example: Her cracked old voice surprised her into giving a cracked old cackle of laughter.

vocabulary.definition: not excited, nervous, or upset
vocabulary.example: She was surprised at how calm she still felt.

vocabulary.definition: to make a long high sound when something moves or is moved
vocabulary.example: Her joints creaked as she moved.

vocabulary.definition: an old woman who is thin and ugly (often used in stories or folklore)
vocabulary.example: Sophie discovered that being a crone did not stop her enjoying the sight and smell of may.

vocabulary.definition: certain to fail, suffer, or die
vocabulary.example: Now if I wasn’t doomed to failure because of my position in the family.

vocabulary.definition: clearly; obviously
vocabulary.example: Evidently her eyes were not as good as they had been.

vocabulary.definition: (especially of an old person) strong and healthy
vocabulary.example: She was quite a hale old woman.

vocabulary.definition: to lift or pull something heavy with a lot of effort
vocabulary.example: Sophie heaved the thing upright.

vocabulary.definition: strong upward or outward movements or pulls
vocabulary.example: Followed by heavings that shook may petals off the hedge.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with difficulty, especially because your feet or legs hurt or are injured
vocabulary.example: She hobbled over to the shop door and carefully put up the CLOSED notice.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that is certain to fail or end badly
vocabulary.example: It was hopelessly trapped by a stout stick.

vocabulary.definition: a small package or wrapped bundle
vocabulary.example: She collected her purse with a few coins in it and a parcel of bread and cheese.

vocabulary.definition: to look carefully or with difficulty
vocabulary.example: Sophie crawled on her bony knees to peer past leaves and flowers and thorns.

vocabulary.definition: in a confident, cheeky, or slightly disreputable way
vocabulary.example: It stood looming rakishly above the may.

vocabulary.definition: to cut something with a saw (or scissors) back and forth
vocabulary.example: She sawed away at the rope round the dog’s neck.

vocabulary.definition: only just; almost not
vocabulary.example: The way I am now, it’s scarcely worth worrying about.

vocabulary.definition: to move quietly and slowly without lifting your feet completely off the ground
vocabulary.example: Then she shuffled through into the house.

vocabulary.definition: (of a part of the body) not moving or working easily
vocabulary.example: She did not feel weak or ill, just stiff.

vocabulary.definition: strong and thick
vocabulary.example: It was hopelessly trapped by a stout stick which had somehow got twisted into a rope.

vocabulary.definition: made a choking or strangling sound
vocabulary.example: There were little strangled squeakings.

vocabulary.definition: in a strong and steady way
vocabulary.example: She hobbled sturdily enough.

vocabulary.definition: old and torn; in poor condition
vocabulary.example: The tattered sleeves on its stick arms fluttering over the hedge.

vocabulary.definition: turned into a shape that is not straight; bent
vocabulary.example: A stout stick which had somehow got twisted into a rope that was tied round the dog's neck.

vocabulary.definition: to force something into a narrow space so that it is fixed there
vocabulary.example: The stick had wedged itself between two branches of the hedge.

vocabulary.definition: (especially of plants) dry and dying or dead
vocabulary.example: It had a withered turnip for a face.
